title: OOF regime specialists with rank-preserving calibration
summary: Different mechanisms drive stops in wet laps, mandatory-compound completion, late-race sprints, and standard dry degradation.
rationale: Different mechanisms drive stops in wet laps, mandatory-compound completion, late-race sprints, and standard dry degradation. A blended specialist setup can exploit this heterogeneity, but calibration should avoid hurting AUC; scikit-learn notes isotonic can change ranking due to ties, while sigmoid preserves ranking.
implementation_hint: Train 3-4 specialists on disjoint regimes such as slick vs wet, early/mid/late race, and low-vs-high field-shock laps; stack only OOF predictions with a simple meta-learner, and if post-calibration is used, prefer sigmoid/Platt on OOF predictions instead of isotonic. Evaluate all blending with race-group holdouts.
expected_effect: Usually small but reliable lift from model diversity and better handling of distinct strategic regimes, with lower risk of AUC erosion from calibration.
risk: Too many regimes can fragment data and create unstable specialists; gains may be marginal unless the gating features are clean.
